content: The moment you try to put a custom struct into a BTreeSet or use the standard sort functions, Rust immediately halts you with an error demanding that your type implement Ord. This is not arbitrary magic; it is a direct consequence of how the compiler enforces contract invariants for ordered collections and sorted iterators. At its core, the Ord trait is simply a guarantee that every pair of elements can be compared to produce one of three outcomes: strictly less than, equal, or strictly greater than, and crucially, this relationship must be transitive and consistent across all comparisons. When you define a new struct like yours with fields such as id and name, the compiler cannot assume these fields are ordered because it does not know what comparison logic you intend to use on that specific combination of data. You must explicitly declare that your type implements Ord by providing an implementation block that defines the compare function using a closure or explicit if-else logic. Without this declaration, any attempt to insert your struct into a BTreeSet will fail because the set requires elements to be naturally comparable to determine which one comes first in the sorted sequence, and similarly, when you call sort_by on a collection of items, it needs an Ordering value from each pair of elements to establish the permutation that results in a sorted state. The most common pitfall occurs when you implement PartialOrd but forget Ord; while PartialOrd allows you to compare two values for equality or less-than relationships without failing if one is missing, BTreeSet and sort algorithms require total ordering where every comparison yields a definitive result. If your struct contains an Option field used as a primary sort key, simply returning true when the option is None will break transitivity because a value with a None might be considered smaller than another value that has a valid number but also happens to be larger in some other dimension. The correct approach is to handle all cases explicitly within your Ord implementation by checking both fields and their respective Option states before deciding whether item A comes before item B or they are equal. Once you have properly implemented the trait, the underlying mechanism becomes clear: the runtime leverages this ordering information to maintain a balanced tree structure in sets and to perform bubble sort or merge sort logic on collections, ensuring that every insertion preserves sorted order and every retrieval returns elements in sequence without requiring manual reordering operations. This is why you cannot rely on default behavior for custom types; the compiler demands explicit proof of total ordering before allowing your type to participate in these specific ecosystem components.
